walkthrough.title: Hand Out Answer Cards and Pens
walkthrough.text: Let's get Wits & Wagers set up. First, give each player or team one dry-erase answer card and a dry-erase pen. These are the small rectangular boards you will write your guesses on. Every answer in this game is a number, so all you need is something to scribble digits on. Make sure each card has a different color or number so you can tell them apart when guesses go on the betting mat.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Betting Chips
walkthrough.text: Now give each player or team two cardboard betting chips. These are the large round chips with a felt backing, and each set matches the color of an answer card. These are yours for the entire game. You never lose them. You will place them on the betting mat each round to show which guess you think is closest to the correct answer. Think of them as your two votes.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Poker Chip Bank
walkthrough.text: Find all the poker chips and put them in a central pile. Red chips are worth 1 point each, and blue chips are worth 5 points each. Choose one person to be the banker. The banker manages the chip supply and handles payouts at the end of each round. Each player or team starts with two red poker chips from the bank. That is your starting bankroll.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Betting Mat
walkthrough.text: Unfold the large betting mat and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This is where answer cards will be sorted and where you will place your bets. You will see numbered payout slots printed on the mat. The center slot pays 2:1, the next slots out pay 3:1, then 4:1, 5:1, and 6:1 on the far edges. There is also a special slot labeled All Guesses Too High.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Question Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the deck of question cards. Shuffle them and draw seven cards face down without looking at them. These seven cards are your game. Stack them in a neat pile near the banker or question reader. Put the rest of the deck back in the box. Every question has a numerical answer, so there are no opinion questions or trick answers to worry about.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Question Reader
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the question reader. This person reads the trivia question aloud each round. The question reader also plays the game and writes their own guess. The question reader and the banker can be the same person if you want to keep things simple. Hand the sand timer to the question reader as well.

walkthrough.title: Form Teams if Needed
walkthrough.text: Wits & Wagers works with 3 to 7 players or teams. If you have more than 7 people, split into teams. Each team shares one answer card, one set of betting chips, and one poker chip bankroll. Teams discuss and agree on a single guess each round. Even with fewer players, you can team up if you want. The game shines with 6 or more players or teams because it fills up more of the betting mat.

summary.title: Hand Out Answer Cards and Pens
bullets: Give each player or team 1 dry-erase answer card and 1 pen
Each card should be a different color or number

summary.title: Distribute Betting Chips
bullets: Give each player or team 2 cardboard betting chips matching their answer card color
These are never lost — you always get them back

summary.title: Set Up the Poker Chip Bank
bullets: Pile all poker chips centrally: Red = 1 point, Blue = 5 points
Choose a banker to manage the supply
Each player or team starts with 2 red poker chips

summary.title: Lay Out the Betting Mat
bullets: Unfold betting mat in center of table
Payout slots: center 2:1, then 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 6:1 outward
Special slot: All Guesses Too High

summary.title: Prepare the Question Cards
bullets: Shuffle question deck and draw 7 cards face down
Stack near the question reader — return the rest to the box

summary.title: Choose a Question Reader
bullets: Pick someone to read questions aloud each round
Question reader also plays the game normally
Give them the sand timer

summary.title: Form Teams if Needed
bullets: 3-7 players or teams; split into teams if more than 7 people
Each team shares 1 answer card, 1 set of betting chips, 1 bankroll
Best with 6+ players or teams to fill the betting mat

walkthrough.title: Read the Question
walkthrough.text: Each round begins with the question reader flipping over the top question card and reading it aloud. Every question has a numerical answer. For example: In what year did the first email get sent? Or: How many bones are in the human body? The reader shows the card to everyone so they can see the question text. No one knows the answer yet, including the reader.

walkthrough.title: Write Your Guess
walkthrough.text: Once the question is read, flip the sand timer. Everyone has 30 seconds to write a numerical guess on their answer card. You do not need to know the right answer. Just write your best guess. It can be a wild guess, an educated estimate, or something you are fairly sure about. Write large and clearly so everyone can read it. When you are done, place your card face down on the table.

walkthrough.title: Sort Guesses on the Betting Mat
walkthrough.text: When the timer runs out, everyone reveals their answer cards. Now sort them on the betting mat from smallest to largest, left to right. If there is an odd number of unique guesses, the middle guess goes on the center 2:1 payout slot. If there is an even number of unique guesses, the 2:1 slot stays empty and the two middle guesses go on the 3:1 slots. Guesses spread outward from the center. If two or more players wrote the same number, those cards share the same slot.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Payout Slots
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the mat. Each slot has a payout ratio printed on it. The center slot pays 2:1, meaning for every chip you bet there, you win 2 chips back. The next slots out pay 3:1, then 4:1, 5:1, and 6:1 at the far edges. The further from center a guess is, the less likely it is correct, but the bigger the payoff. There is also the All Guesses Too High slot at one end, which pays 6:1 if every single guess overshoots the real answer.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Bets
walkthrough.text: Now flip the sand timer again for another 30 seconds. This is the betting phase. You have your two cardboard betting chips. Place them on any slot or slots on the betting mat. You can put both chips on the same slot, or split them across two different slots. You can bet on any guess, not just your own. If you want, stack poker chips underneath your betting chips to increase your wager. The poker chips you place are at risk. If the slot loses, you lose those poker chips.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Answer
walkthrough.text: The question reader flips the question card over to reveal the correct answer. Read it aloud so everyone can hear. Now find the winning slot on the mat. The winning guess is the one closest to the correct answer without going over. This is crucial — without going over. If the correct answer is 206 and guesses are 150, 200, and 250, then 200 wins because 250 went over. If every single guess is higher than the correct answer, then the All Guesses Too High slot wins.

walkthrough.title: Pay Out Winnings
walkthrough.text: The banker now handles payouts. Look at the winning slot and its payout ratio. Every betting chip on that slot earns its payout. If you placed 3 poker chips under your betting chip on a 3:1 slot, you get 9 poker chips back from the bank, plus your original 3 poker chips. Your cardboard betting chips are always returned to you regardless of whether you won or lost. Poker chips on losing slots go to the bank.

walkthrough.title: Bonus for Correct Guessers
walkthrough.text: Here is a nice consolation for good guessing. If your answer card is sitting on the winning slot, you earn a bonus of 3 red poker chips from the bank, no matter where you placed your bets. So even if you bet on a different slot, you still get 3 chips for having the closest guess. If multiple players share the winning slot because they wrote the same guess, each of them gets the 3 chip bonus.

walkthrough.title: Clean Up for the Next Round
walkthrough.text: Everyone picks up their answer cards, wipes them clean with a sleeve or cloth, and collects their two cardboard betting chips. Organize your poker chip stash. The banker should have the chip supply ready for next round. Then the question reader draws the next card and you do it all again.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Winning
walkthrough.text: After all 7 questions have been played, the game is over. Everyone counts up their poker chips. Red chips are 1 point each and blue chips are 5 points each. The player or team with the most points wins. If there is a tie, the youngest player or the team with the youngest member wins. That is the entire game. Seven rounds of guessing, betting, and laughing.

Even # unique guesses: 2:1 empty, two middles on 3:1 slots

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a full practice round with six players. We have Player 1 through Player 6, each with their own answer card, pen, and two cardboard betting chips. Everyone starts with 2 red poker chips. Player 1 is the question reader, and Player 2 is the banker. The betting mat is laid out in the center of the table with all the payout slots visible.

walkthrough.title: Question 1 Is Read
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws the first question card and reads it aloud: How many countries are in the United Nations? The sand timer is flipped. Everyone has 30 seconds to write a guess. Remember, you do not need to know the answer. Just write a number that feels reasonable.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Writes a Guess
walkthrough.text: The players scribble their guesses. Player 1 writes 150. Player 2 writes 180. Player 3 writes 220. Player 4 writes 193. Player 5 writes 193 as well. Player 6 writes 160. Time is up. Everyone flips their cards face up.

walkthrough.title: Sorting the Guesses
walkthrough.text: Now we sort all the guesses on the betting mat from smallest to largest. We have six guesses but only five unique values because Player 4 and Player 5 both wrote 193. So the order is: 150, 160, 180, 193, 220. Five unique guesses is an odd number, so the middle guess goes on the center 2:1 slot. The middle of five is 180, so Player 2's card goes on the 2:1. Then 160 goes on the 3:1 slot to the left, 193 goes on the 3:1 slot to the right. Player 4 and Player 5's cards stack together on that same 3:1 slot since they are duplicates. Then 150 goes on the 4:1 slot to the left, and 220 goes on the 4:1 slot to the right.

walkthrough.title: Time to Bet
walkthrough.text: The sand timer flips again. Thirty seconds to place bets. Everyone studies the mat. The actual number of UN member states is 193. Of course, nobody knows that yet, but some players have a hunch. Player 5 remembers hearing it was around 190, so they feel pretty confident about the 193 slot.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 Makes a Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is sure their guess of 220 is right. They put both of their cardboard betting chips on the 220 slot at the 4:1 payout and stack their entire bankroll of 2 red poker chips under one of the betting chips. Player 3 is going all in on themselves. This is risky. By putting both chips on the same slot, Player 3 has no safety net. If 220 is wrong, they lose everything they wagered. Splitting bets across two slots would have been much safer.

walkthrough.title: Smarter Bets from Other Players
walkthrough.text: Player 5 puts one betting chip on the 193 slot at 3:1 with 1 red poker chip underneath, and their other betting chip on the 180 slot at 2:1 with 1 red chip underneath. Player 5 is hedging. They think 193 is right, but if it is a bit lower, 180 would be closest without going over. Player 1 puts both chips on 180. Player 4 puts one chip on 193 and one on 180. Player 6 splits between 160 and 180. Player 2 puts both chips on their own guess of 180.

walkthrough.title: The Answer Is Revealed
walkthrough.text: Player 1 flips the question card over. The answer is 193. The table erupts. Now we find the winning slot. The closest guess without going over is 193, which is sitting on the 3:1 slot to the right. That is the winning slot. Notice that 220 went over, so Player 3's guess loses even though it is only 27 away. In Wits & Wagers, going over means you are out, no matter how close you were.

walkthrough.title: Paying Out Round 1
walkthrough.text: The banker handles payouts. The winning slot is the 3:1 on the right where 193 sits. Player 5 had 1 red chip wagered there, so they win 3 red chips from the bank and keep their original 1 red chip. Player 4 also had a betting chip on 193 with no poker chips underneath, so they win nothing extra but do not lose anything either — cardboard betting chips are always free to place. Player 3 loses both red poker chips they wagered on 220. They are now broke. Everyone else who bet on non-winning slots loses their wagered poker chips too.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Chips Awarded
walkthrough.text: Now the bonus. Player 4 and Player 5 both had their answer cards on the winning 193 slot. Each of them receives 3 red poker chips as a bonus from the bank. That is a nice reward just for guessing well, even separate from any bets. Player 3 gets nothing extra because 220 was not on the winning slot. After payouts and bonuses, let's count up: Player 5 has 8 red chips total and is in the lead. Player 4 has 6. Player 3 is sitting at 0.

walkthrough.title: What Player 3 Should Have Done
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about Player 3's mistake. They put both betting chips on their own guess and wagered their entire bankroll. The problem is twofold. First, by not splitting bets, they had zero chance of winning anything unless their exact slot hit. Second, they ignored the table. Player 4 and Player 5 both wrote 193, suggesting they had some knowledge. When two people independently agree on a number, that is a strong signal. Player 3 should have at least put one chip on 193 as a hedge.

walkthrough.title: Question 2 Begins
walkthrough.text: Round 2. Player 1 reads the next question: How many miles long is the Great Wall of China? Sand timer flips. Guesses come in. Player 1 writes 3,000. Player 2 writes 5,000. Player 3 writes 4,000. Player 4 writes 13,000. Player 5 writes 8,000. Player 6 writes 6,000. Cards are revealed and sorted on the mat.

walkthrough.title: Sorting and Betting Round 2
walkthrough.text: Six unique guesses this time, which is even. So the 2:1 center slot stays empty. The two middle guesses, 5,000 and 6,000, go on the two 3:1 slots. Then 4,000 and 8,000 go on the 4:1 slots, 3,000 and 13,000 go on the 5:1 slots. Betting begins. Player 3 has no poker chips to wager, so they just place their two free cardboard betting chips. They split them across 5,000 and 6,000. Lesson learned. Player 5, sitting on 8 red chips, bets aggressively: 3 red chips on 13,000 at 5:1 with one betting chip, and 1 red chip on 8,000 at 4:1 with the other.

walkthrough.title: The Answer and Payouts for Round 2
walkthrough.text: The answer is revealed: 13,170 miles. Player 4's guess of 13,000 is the closest without going over. It is on the 5:1 slot. Player 5 had bet 3 red chips on that 13,000 slot. At 5:1, that pays 15 red chips from the bank, plus the original 3 back. Player 5 now has a mountain of chips. Player 4 gets the 3 red chip bonus for having their answer card on the winning slot. Player 3, who bet on 5,000 and 6,000, gets nothing this round but at least did not lose any chips since they had none to wager.

walkthrough.title: The Chip Landscape After 2 Rounds
walkthrough.text: Let's look at chip totals after just two rounds. Player 5 is dominating with over 20 chips thanks to smart hedging and one big 5:1 payout. Player 4 has a solid 9 or so chips from bonuses and careful play. Player 3 is at zero but still in the game. They still have their two free cardboard betting chips every round. Five more rounds to go, and a single big payout on a high-odds slot can change everything. Nobody is ever eliminated in Wits & Wagers.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways from the Practice
walkthrough.text: Here is what to take away from these two rounds. One: you do not need to know the answer. Betting smart matters more than guessing right. Two: split your bets. Hedging across two slots keeps you in the game even when your favorite slot loses. Three: watch what other people write. If two players independently guess the same number, that slot is worth a bet. Four: the bonus for guessing correctly is real money. Three free chips every time your card is on the winning slot adds up over seven rounds.

walkthrough.title: You Are Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is everything you need to know to play Wits & Wagers Deluxe. Seven rounds, seven questions, and a whole lot of laughing at the ridiculous guesses people come up with. Before you start your real game, check out General Tips for quick strategy advice and the Appendix for a reference on all the components and terms. And remember, you can always tap the Q&A tab to ask your personal GM Guide any question during the game. Have fun and bet wisely!

walkthrough.title: You Don't Need to Know Trivia
walkthrough.text: This is the most important thing to understand about Wits & Wagers. You do not need to be a trivia expert. In fact, most winning players are not the ones who guess correctly the most. They are the ones who bet wisely. Your job is to figure out which guess on the mat is most likely to be correct and put your chips there. Think of yourself as a poker player reading the table, not a contestant on a quiz show.

walkthrough.title: Bet on the Expert at the Table
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what people write. If someone at the table is a history buff and the question is about a historical date, their guess is probably closer than yours. If the science nerd writes a number for a biology question, take that seriously. You are allowed to bet on anyone's guess, so use the people around you as a resource. The best Wits & Wagers players are great at identifying who knows what.

walkthrough.title: Always Split Your Bets
walkthrough.text: You have two betting chips. Use both of them, and put them on different slots. Splitting your bets across two adjacent guesses is the safest play. If you think the answer is somewhere around 200, and the guesses near 200 are on the 2:1 and 3:1 slots, put one chip on each. You will win on at least one if you are in the right neighborhood. Going all in on a single slot only makes sense if you are extremely confident.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Odds
walkthrough.text: The payout ratios are there for a reason. A guess on the 2:1 center slot is the most likely to be correct because it is the median guess. But it also pays the least. A guess on a 5:1 slot is a long shot, but it pays five times your wager. Think about risk and reward. If you have a lot of chips, a small bet on a long-shot slot can pay off enormously. If you are low on chips, stick to the safer center bets to stay in the game.

walkthrough.title: Don't Always Bet on Yourself
walkthrough.text: New players almost always put their chips on their own guess. Resist this urge. Your guess might be way off, and you should evaluate it objectively. If your guess ended up on a 5:1 slot at the far edge, that means most other players thought the answer was very different from what you wrote. That is useful information. The crowd is often wiser than any individual. Be honest with yourself about when someone else probably has a better guess.

walkthrough.title: Go Big in Later Rounds
walkthrough.text: In the first couple of rounds, everyone has very few poker chips. The bets are small and the swings are limited. But by rounds 5, 6, and 7, the chip leaders will have accumulated a sizable bankroll. This is when the game gets exciting. If you are behind, later rounds are your chance to make a big bet on a high-odds slot and close the gap. If you are ahead, you can afford to take calculated risks because you have chips to spare. The last round especially is where fortunes are won and lost.

walkthrough.title: Remember the Closest Without Going Over Rule
walkthrough.text: This is not a game about being close to the answer. It is a game about being close without going over. A guess that is 1 over the correct answer loses to a guess that is 1,000 under. This changes how you should think about your guesses. When in doubt, guess a little lower than you think the answer might be. A conservative guess that lands under the answer has a real chance of winning. An aggressive guess that overshoots is dead.

bullets: Winning comes from smart betting, not trivia knowledge
Read the table like a poker player, not a quiz contestant

bullets: Watch who writes what — bet on the person likely to know
You can bet on anyone's guess, not just your own

Only go all-in on one slot if you are extremely confident

summary.title: Watch the Odds

Edge slots (5:1, 6:1) are long shots but pay big
Balance risk and reward based on your chip stack

summary.title: Don't Always Bet on Yourself

If your guess is on a far-edge slot, the crowd disagrees with you
The crowd is often wiser than any individual

summary.title: Go Big in Later Rounds
bullets: Early rounds have small bets and small swings
Rounds 5-7 are where chip leaders can bet aggressively
If behind, a big bet on a high-odds slot can close the gap

summary.title: Remember the Closest Without Going Over Rule
bullets: 1 over the answer loses to 1,000 under the answer
When in doubt, guess conservatively (lower)
Overshooting is dead — undershooting stays alive

walkthrough.title: Read the Room for Expertise
walkthrough.text: Before the game even starts, take mental notes about your group. Who works in science? Who is a sports fan? Who travels a lot? Who watches too many documentaries? When a question comes up about geography, you already know whose guess to take seriously. Advanced players build a mental profile of each person at the table and update it after every round based on how close their guesses were. By round 4 or 5, you will know who the reliable guesser is for each category of question.

walkthrough.title: Hedge Your Bets Across Adjacent Slots
walkthrough.text: The most mathematically sound strategy is to place your two betting chips on two adjacent slots. If the answer falls anywhere in that range, you win on one of them. For example, if you think the answer is between two guesses sitting on the 2:1 and 3:1 slots, cover both. Your expected payout is better than going all in on either one alone. Think of it like insurance. You are paying a small opportunity cost to protect yourself against a near miss.

walkthrough.title: Calculate Expected Value
walkthrough.text: Every slot has a payout ratio, and you can loosely estimate the probability of each slot winning based on the guesses you see. If you think a 3:1 slot has about a 40 percent chance of winning, its expected value per chip wagered is 1.2 chips, which is positive. If a 5:1 slot has only a 10 percent chance, its expected value is 0.5, which is negative. You do not need to do exact math at the table, but developing a gut feel for expected value will help you avoid sucker bets on long shots and identify when a high-odds slot is actually undervalued.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Go All In
walkthrough.text: Going all in means stacking a large portion of your poker chips under one betting chip on a single slot. This is high risk, high reward. The right time to go all in is when you have strong information and the payout justifies the risk. For example, if a doctor at the table writes a guess for a medical question and it lands on a 3:1 slot, that is a great all-in opportunity. The wrong time is when you are guessing blind on a topic nobody knows anything about. Also consider your position in the game. If you are far behind in the last two rounds, going big is your only path to winning. If you are leading, protect your stack.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Bankroll
walkthrough.text: Think of your poker chips as a bankroll that needs to last seven rounds. If you blow everything on round 1 and lose, you are stuck placing free betting chips for the rest of the game. A good rule of thumb is to wager no more than one-third of your chips in any single round during the first four rounds. Save your bigger bets for rounds 5 through 7 when you have more information about who guesses well and you need to make a move for the win or protect a lead.

walkthrough.title: Bluff with Your Guesses
walkthrough.text: Here is a subtle psychological tactic. Your guess does not just affect where your card lands on the mat. It affects where everyone else bets. If you confidently and quickly write down a number, other players might assume you know the answer and pile their bets on your slot. You can use this to your advantage. Write a guess that you think is reasonable, but then bet on a different slot where you think the real answer is closer. Other players follow your guess while you quietly cash in somewhere else. This works best when you have built a reputation as a good guesser earlier in the game.

walkthrough.title: Exploit the All Guesses Too High Slot
walkthrough.text: Most players ignore the All Guesses Too High slot because it feels unlikely. But for certain types of questions, especially ones where humans tend to overestimate, it is a real threat. Questions about how few of something exist or how small a measurement is often produce inflated guesses from the whole table. If you see a question like How many calories are in a plain rice cake, and every guess on the mat is above 100, it might be worth tossing a chip on the Too High slot at 6:1. A small speculative bet there can pay off enormously.

walkthrough.title: Track Chip Counts in Late Rounds
walkthrough.text: In the final two rounds, knowing how many chips each player has is crucial. If you are in second place and the leader has 10 more chips than you, you need to figure out a scenario where you catch up. That might mean betting on a different slot than the leader so a win creates separation. Or it might mean betting on the same slot to neutralize their bet and hope your bonus guess chips close the gap. Advanced players treat the last round like a final poker hand. They calculate what they need to win and bet accordingly, not randomly.

summary.title: Read the Room for Expertise
bullets: Profile each player's knowledge areas before the game starts
Update your mental model after each round based on accuracy
By round 4-5, you will know who to trust for each topic

summary.title: Hedge Your Bets Across Adjacent Slots
bullets: Two chips on two adjacent slots = best coverage
If the answer is in range, you win on at least one
Think of it as insurance against near misses

summary.title: Calculate Expected Value
bullets: Expected value = payout ratio x probability of winning
A 3:1 slot at 40% chance = 1.2 EV per chip (good bet)
A 5:1 slot at 10% chance = 0.5 EV per chip (bad bet)
Develop gut feel for when long shots are undervalued

summary.title: Know When to Go All In
bullets: Go all in when you have strong info + good payout ratio
Behind in late rounds: big bets are your only path to winning
Leading: protect your stack, avoid unnecessary risk

summary.title: Manage Your Bankroll
bullets: Wager no more than 1/3 of chips in rounds 1-4
Save bigger bets for rounds 5-7
Going broke early means only free betting chips for the rest of the game

summary.title: Bluff with Your Guesses
bullets: Write a confident guess to attract bets to your slot
Then bet on a different slot where you think the real answer is
Works best after you have built a reputation as a good guesser

summary.title: Exploit the All Guesses Too High Slot
bullets: Questions about small quantities often produce inflated guesses
A speculative chip on Too High at 6:1 can pay off big
Most players ignore this slot, creating an opportunity

summary.title: Track Chip Counts in Late Rounds
bullets: Know how many chips each player has in rounds 6-7
Bet strategically relative to the leader's position
Treat the last round like a final poker hand — calculate what you need

entries.definition: The large foldable mat placed in the center of the table. Answer cards are sorted on it from smallest to largest, and payout slots are printed along the center. All bets are placed directly on the mat.
Dry-erase cards used by each player or team to write their numerical guess each round. Each card is a different color or number for identification. Wiped clean between rounds.
Markers included with the game for writing guesses on the answer cards. Each player or team gets one pen.

Two large round felt-backed chips given to each player or team. Used to mark which slot you are betting on. These are always returned to you after each round regardless of outcome. They cost nothing to place.

Poker chips worth 1 point each. Wagered under cardboard betting chips to increase your bet. Lost if your slot does not win, paid out at the slot's ratio if it does. Also awarded as bonuses for correct guesses.
Poker chips worth 5 points each. Function identically to red chips but at higher value. Used when a player accumulates enough red chips to exchange up, or during large wagers.
Cards with a trivia question on one side and the numerical answer on the other. Seven cards are drawn at the start of the game, one per round. All answers are numbers.
A 30-second timer flipped twice per round. Once during the guessing phase and once during the betting phase. Keeps the game moving at a brisk pace.

The center slot on the betting mat. Pays 2 chips for every 1 chip wagered. The median guess is placed here when there is an odd number of unique guesses. Left empty when the number of unique guesses is even.
The two slots immediately flanking the center. Pay 3 chips for every 1 chip wagered. When the center is empty (even number of guesses), the two middle guesses land here.
The next pair of slots outward from the 3:1 slots. Pay 4 chips for every 1 chip wagered. Guesses here are further from the median and less likely to win, but the payout is larger.
The next pair of slots outward from the 4:1 slots. Pay 5 chips for every 1 chip wagered. Long-shot bets that can yield big returns.
The outermost pair of slots on the betting mat. Pay 6 chips for every 1 chip wagered. The least likely guesses to win, but the highest payout in the game.
A special slot on the betting mat that wins when every single guess on the mat exceeds the correct answer. Pays 6:1. Often overlooked but valuable for questions where overestimation is common.

The core rule for determining the winning guess. The winning answer is the one nearest to the correct answer that does not exceed it. A guess that is even 1 over the correct answer cannot win, no matter how close it is.
The first phase of each round. The question is read aloud, the 30-second timer is flipped, and all players write a numerical guess on their answer card. Cards are placed face down until time is up.

The second phase of each round. After guesses are sorted on the mat, the 30-second timer is flipped again. Players place their two cardboard betting chips (and optional poker chip wagers) on any slot(s).
When two or more players write the same number, their answer cards share a single slot on the betting mat. Both players are eligible for the 3-chip bonus if that slot wins.
A designated player who manages the poker chip supply, handles payouts after each round, and distributes bonus chips. The banker also plays the game normally.
A designated player who reads each question card aloud and manages the sand timer. The question reader also plays the game normally and can be the same person as the banker.
A reward of 3 red poker chips given to any player whose answer card is on the winning slot, regardless of where they placed their bets. All players sharing the winning slot receive this bonus.

When playing with more than 7 people, players form teams. Each team shares one answer card, one set of betting chips, and one poker chip bankroll. Team members discuss and agree on a single guess each round.

After 7 rounds, all players count their poker chips. Red chips = 1 point, blue chips = 5 points. The player or team with the most total points wins. Tiebreaker goes to the youngest player or team with the youngest member.
